one_line_takeaway: A taxonomy-style survey scoring ~80 DRL autonomous-driving decision-making papers across four scenarios on five 'rational' criteria (safety, efficiency, training efficiency, unselfishness, interpretability), finding efficiency over-emphasized (77.8%) and unselfishness/interpretability neglected (<40%) with almost no method covering all five; it is an operational-safety survey, not an AI-security paper.
key_finding: Coverage of the five factors is highly uneven, and holistic frameworks covering all five simultaneously are rare. Across all reviewed studies, driving efficiency is the most commonly addressed factor (63 studies, 77.8%) and unselfishness the least (29 studies, 35.8%). Efficiency dominates at intersections (17/18 studies, 94.4%) and ramps (14/16, 87.5%); safety is most emphasized on highways (23/30, 76.7%) but least at intersections (5/18, 27.7%); training efficiency is emphasized most at roundabouts (12/16, 75%). Interpretability is addressed in under 40% of papers, and very few studies integrate all five factors (only 3 of 16 roundabout studies and 1 of 19 intersection studies). Emerging trends the authors highlight are multi-agent RL (MARL) for unselfishness (~50% of unselfish studies) and hybridizing DRL with classical control/models (e.g., IDM, NMPC, APF) for interpretability.
core_contribution: A survey that reviews and taxonomically evaluates deep-reinforcement-learning (DRL) decision-making algorithms for interactive autonomous driving, organized by four typical road scenarios (highways, on-ramp merging, roundabouts, unsignalized intersections) and scored against five 'rational criteria' abbreviated DDTUI: Driving safety, Driving efficiency, Training efficiency, Unselfishness, and Interpretability. The authors' own contribution is (1) the DDTUI evaluation framework and its rationale, (2) a per-paper mapping of ~80 reviewed DRL studies to which of the five factors each addresses AND verifies (Tables I-IV), (3) an aggregate occurrence/ratio analysis of the five factors across scenarios (Table V), and (4) three identified research gaps. It is a literature survey with no new algorithm, dataset, or experiment of its own.
threat_model: Not applicable. This is not a security paper. 'Safety' here means operational collision avoidance and traffic-flow quality (e.g., collisions with surrounding human-driven vehicles, maintaining a safe following distance ds, time-to-collision thresholds), not adversarial robustness, cyberattacks, sensor spoofing, or any malicious threat model. The 'Autonomous Vehicle Security' cluster label is a misclassification; the paper never discusses an adversary, attack surface, or defense. No threat model is invented here.
limitations: It is a qualitative survey: the DDTUI 'evaluation' is a binary addressed/not-addressed coding per paper, not a quantitative benchmark, so reviewed algorithms are never run against a common metric or dataset by the authors. The addressed/verified judgment is subjective and not backed by an inter-rater reliability check. Scenario study counts are modest (30 highway, 16 ramp, 16 roundabout, 18-19 intersection) and the corpus mixes traffic-signal/ramp-metering control works with ego-vehicle decision-making. The 'five rational criteria' are asserted rather than empirically validated as complete or independent. As already noted, despite the security cluster label the paper contains no security/adversarial content.

quantitative_results: Overall factor coverage across all reviewed studies (Table V): Safety 47 (58%), Efficiency 63 (77.8%), Training Efficiency 44 (54.3%), Unselfishness 29 (35.8%), Interpretability 32 (39.5%)
Highway scenario (Table V, ~30 studies): Safety 23 (76.7%), Efficiency 20 (66.7%), Training Efficiency 11 (36.7%), Unselfishness 13 (43.3%), Interpretability 11 (36.7%)
Ramp scenario (~16 studies): Safety 9 (56.25%), Efficiency 14 (87.5%), Training Efficiency 8 (50%), Unselfishness 8 (50%), Interpretability 9 (56.25%)
Roundabout scenario (~16 studies): Safety 10 (62.5%), Efficiency 11 (68.75%), Training Efficiency 12 (75%), Unselfishness 3 (18.75%), Interpretability 6 (37.5%)
Intersection scenario (~18 studies): Safety 5 (27.7%), Efficiency 17 (94.4%), Training Efficiency 12 (66.7%), Unselfishness 4 (22.2%), Interpretability 6 (33.3%)

All-five-factor coverage is rare: only 3 of 16 roundabout studies and 1 of 19 intersection studies address all five DDTUI factors; fewer than 40% of reviewed papers address interpretability; roughly 50% of unselfishness studies rely on MARL

Motivating statistics cited from other works (not the authors' results): over 900 Tesla driver-assistance crashes since 2021 [ref 2]; AV count projected to exceed 50 million by 2024 [ref 3]; in ADS-HDV rear-end collisions, 79% (252 of 319) were HDV hitting the ADS vs 21% (67) ADS hitting HDV [ref 14/16]
